content.text: Continued pre-training (CPT) is the process of resuming the original language modelling training objective on a new data distribution, starting from an existing model checkpoint rather than from random weights. The model has already learned general linguistic structure, world knowledge, and reasoning patterns from its original pre-training corpus. CPT extends or redirects that knowledge without paying the full cost of training from scratch.
The loss function does not change — CPT uses the same next-token prediction (causal language modelling) objective as the original pre-training run. What changes is the
(10× to 1000× fewer than a full pre-training run).

attrs.alt: Three-stage model development: pre-training from scratch → continued pre-training → task fine-tuning, with data mixture strategies and notable CPT models

content.text: Why Continued Pre-Training Instead of Fine-Tuning

Task fine-tuning on a small labelled dataset updates all or part of the model on a narrow distribution. When the target domain's language differs significantly from the original pre-training corpus — medical records with dense clinical abbreviations, legal contracts with specialised syntax, or code in a niche programming language — the model's tokeniser and internal representations may not serve it well, regardless of how much labelled data you have.

: by training on a large unlabelled corpus of domain text, the model's internal representations shift to encode domain vocabulary, entity relationships, and writing conventions. Subsequent task fine-tuning then works on richer representations and typically achieves higher accuracy with less labelled data — or equal accuracy with a smaller model.
: adapt a general LLM to biomedical, legal, financial, or scientific text. The Gururangan et al. (2020) DAPT paper shows consistent downstream gains across four domains.

: train further on code repositories and documentation to produce models like Code LLaMA (started from LLaMA-2, trained on 500B code tokens). General LLMs with CPT on code significantly outperform same-size models trained on code from scratch.

: extend an English-dominant model to a new language by continued training on the target language's text. This is cheaper than multilingual pre-training from scratch and yields a model that retains English capability while gaining the new language.
: refresh a model's knowledge with recent events or newly available data, without running a full pre-training. This is increasingly relevant as base model cutoffs age.

: LLaMA-2's position embeddings support 4096 tokens. Code LLaMA extends this to 100k via CPT with longer sequences and RoPE (Rotary Position Embedding) rescaling, using documents that specifically exercise long-range dependencies.
CPT uses the same training loop as original pre-training, with three key differences: starting from a saved checkpoint, using a lower learning rate, and running for far fewer total tokens.

from transformers import (
    AutoModelForCausalLM,
    AutoTokenizer,
    TrainingArguments,
    Trainer,
    DataCollatorForLanguageModeling,
)
from datasets import load_dataset
import torch

# 1. Load existing checkpoint (not from_config — from full weights)
model_id = "meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf"
model    = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    model_id,
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    attn_implementation="flash_attention_2",
)
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token  # causal LM has no pad token by default

# 2. Load domain corpus (here: PubMed abstracts)
dataset = load_dataset("pubmed", split="train", streaming=True)

def tokenize(examples):
    return tokenizer(
        examples["MedlineCitation.Article.Abstract.AbstractText"],
        truncation=True,
        max_length=2048,
    )

tokenized = dataset.map(tokenize, batched=True, remove_columns=dataset.column_names)

# 3. Training arguments: lower LR, short warmup, bf16
training_args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir="cpt-biomedical",
    num_train_epochs=1,
    per_device_train_batch_size=4,
    gradient_accumulation_steps=8,   # effective batch = 32
    learning_rate=2e-5,              # 10-50× lower than original pre-training
    warmup_steps=500,                # fresh warmup from checkpoint
    lr_scheduler_type="cosine",
    bf16=True,
    gradient_checkpointing=True,     # trades compute for memory
    logging_steps=100,
    save_steps=5000,
    max_steps=50_000,                # ~3.3B tokens at bs=32, seq=2048
)

# 4. CLM data collator: autoregressive, no masking (unlike MLM)
collator = DataCollatorForLanguageModeling(tokenizer=tokenizer, mlm=False)

trainer = Trainer(
    model=model,
    args=training_args,
    train_dataset=tokenized,
    data_collator=collator,
)
trainer.train()
The quality of the CPT corpus determines the quality of the adapted model. Data preparation has three steps:

: exact and near-duplicate removal using MinHash LSH or URL-based dedup. Duplicates inflate token counts and cause the model to memorise rather than generalise. The Llama-2 pre-training report found that document-level deduplication is more important than token-level.

: remove boilerplate (navigation menus, cookie notices), short documents (< 200 tokens), and documents with high symbol-to-word ratios. For scientific text, keep full-text papers; drop abstract-only records if full text is available.
: include 10–30% of general text (e.g. C4 or SlimPajama). This counteracts catastrophic forgetting of general language understanding. Code LLaMA's training mix included both code and English text.

from datasketch import MinHash, MinHashLSH

def build_minhash(text: str, num_perm: int = 128) -> MinHash:
    m = MinHash(num_perm=num_perm)
    for word in text.lower().split():
        m.update(word.encode("utf8"))
    return m

def deduplicate(documents: list[str], threshold: float = 0.8) -> list[str]:
    """Remove near-duplicates using MinHash LSH (Jaccard ≥ threshold)."""
    lsh = MinHashLSH(threshold=threshold, num_perm=128)
    unique = []
    for i, doc in enumerate(documents):
        mh = build_minhash(doc)
        if not lsh.query(mh):   # no near-duplicate found
            lsh.insert(str(i), mh)
            unique.append(doc)
    return unique

def quality_filter(doc: str, min_words: int = 50) -> bool:
    words = doc.split()
    symbol_ratio = sum(1 for c in doc if not c.isalpha()) / max(len(doc), 1)
    return len(words) >= min_words and symbol_ratio < 0.3
Large LR would destroy pre-trained weights; smaller LR retains structure
Fresh warmup avoids gradient shock at new distribution start
Diminishing returns beyond ~100B for most domains; check perplexity curve
Smaller absolute batch is fine; gradient noise is acceptable at low LR
Don't decay to 0: leaves room for subsequent task FT
If extending context (e.g. 4096 → 100k), use RoPE scaling trick

CPT on a highly specialised corpus risks making the model forget the general language understanding, reasoning, and instruction-following behaviour it acquired during original pre-training. The magnitude of forgetting depends on:
How different the new domain is from the original corpus (code vs medical vs general web)
The proportion of new vs general text in the CPT corpus (0% general = highest forgetting risk)
The number of tokens trained on (more tokens = more shift)
The learning rate (higher LR = faster and more complete forgetting)
stop CPT before general benchmark scores significantly degrade
(monitor at regular checkpoints). PEFT approaches (LoRA CPT) avoid forgetting structurally by leaving base weights unchanged.

Full CPT requires the same GPU memory as full fine-tuning. For a 7B model in bf16 with Adam optimiser, that is roughly 7B × (2 + 8) bytes ≈ 70 GB for weights + optimiser state alone. LoRA CPT keeps the base model frozen and trains only the adapter matrices, reducing GPU requirements dramatically:

from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model, TaskType

# For CPT, use higher rank and include FFN modules
# (domain shift requires updating feed-forward knowledge store,
# not just attention routing)
cpt_lora_config = LoraConfig(
    r=64,                    # higher rank for broad domain shift
    lora_alpha=128,
    target_modules=[
        "q_proj", "k_proj", "v_proj", "o_proj",  # attention
        "gate_proj", "up_proj", "down_proj",       # FFN (SwiGLU)
    ],
    lora_dropout=0.0,        # no dropout for CLM pre-training
    bias="none",
    task_type=TaskType.CAUSAL_LM,
)
model = get_peft_model(model, cpt_lora_config)

# Same Trainer/DataCollatorForLanguageModeling setup as full CPT
# but GPU memory footprint is ~8 GB instead of ~70 GB for 7B model
trainer = Trainer(
    model=model,
    args=training_args,
    train_dataset=tokenized_domain_corpus,
    data_collator=DataCollatorForLanguageModeling(tokenizer, mlm=False),
)
trainer.train()

The trade-off is that LoRA CPT achieves a smaller effective domain shift than full CPT because the base model's representations are frozen — the adapter can only modulate them. For strong domain shift (e.g. training an English model on Chinese), full CPT or vocabulary extension is preferable. For moderate domain shift (e.g. general model → biomedical English), LoRA CPT typically achieves 80–90% of the full CPT performance gain.
Track two metrics throughout CPT training to confirm the adaptation is proceeding correctly:
on a held-out set of domain text. Should decrease monotonically. Plateau or increase signals that the corpus is exhausted or the LR is too high.
(HellaSwag, ARC, MMLU subset). Should remain stable. A drop of > 5% is a signal to increase the proportion of general text in the mix or reduce LR.

import math
import torch
from torch.nn import CrossEntropyLoss
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
from datasets import load_dataset

def domain_perplexity(model_path: str, domain: str = "pubmed") -> float:
    """Compute held-out perplexity on domain text."""
    model     = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_path, torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16)
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_path)
    model.eval()

    # Use 500 held-out abstracts as validation set
    texts = load_dataset("pubmed", split="validation").select(range(500))
    total_loss, total_tokens = 0.0, 0

    with torch.no_grad():
        for example in texts:
            ids = tokenizer(
                example["MedlineCitation.Article.Abstract.AbstractText"],
                return_tensors="pt",
                truncation=True, max_length=2048
            )["input_ids"]
            out  = model(input_ids=ids, labels=ids)
            total_loss   += out.loss.item() * ids.numel()
            total_tokens += ids.numel()

    return math.exp(total_loss / total_tokens)

# Call at each checkpoint during CPT to track adaptation progress
print(f"Perplexity: {domain_perplexity('cpt-biomedical/checkpoint-5000'):.2f}")
CPT vs Domain Adaptation vs Task Fine-Tuning
Rather than mixing domain and general data at a fixed ratio throughout training, a

varies the domain proportion across training. This is particularly effective when the target domain is highly specialised — the model first stabilises on mixed data, then increasingly focuses on domain text:

Within the domain corpus itself, complexity ordering can help — simpler, shorter documents (textbook introductions, FAQ entries) before long, dense technical papers. This mirrors how humans learn a field: overview before depth. For code CPT, training on documentation and README files before raw source code is a common pattern.

from torch.utils.data import ConcatDataset, Subset

def curriculum_datasets(domain_data, general_data, step: int, total_steps: int):
    """
    Linear curriculum: increase domain fraction from 50% to 90% over training.
    """
    progress = step / total_steps
    domain_frac = 0.50 + 0.40 * progress  # 50% → 90%
    general_frac = 1.0 - domain_frac

    n_domain  = int(len(domain_data)  * domain_frac)
    n_general = int(len(general_data) * general_frac)

    # Re-sample each step call is expensive in practice;
    # implement as epoch-level dataset reconstruction instead
    mixed = ConcatDataset([
        Subset(domain_data,  range(n_domain)),
        Subset(general_data, range(n_general)),
    ])
    return mixed

A 7B parameter model in bf16 requires ~14 GB for weights alone. The Adam optimiser adds two momentum buffers — bringing the total to ~42 GB. Gradient activations for a batch add another 10–30 GB. Practical CPT at scale requires all of the following:
: halves weight memory vs fp32. Preferred over fp16 for training because bf16 has a wider dynamic range and avoids the gradient underflow that plagues fp16 at large scale. Use
: trades compute for memory by recomputing activations during the backward pass instead of storing them. Reduces activation memory by 4–8× at the cost of ~30% slower training. Enable with
: a fused kernel implementation of scaled dot-product attention that is 2–4× faster and uses O(√n) memory instead of O(n²) for the attention matrix. Set
: quantises the Adam momentum buffers to 8 bits, cutting optimiser state memory from 16 bytes/param to 4 bytes/param. Use

# Memory-optimised training configuration for CPT on a single 80GB A100
training_args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir="cpt-output",
    bf16=True,                          # bf16 activations
    gradient_checkpointing=True,        # recompute activations
    per_device_train_batch_size=2,      # small per-device batch
    gradient_accumulation_steps=32,     # effective batch = 64
    optim="adamw_bnb_8bit",             # 8-bit Adam
    learning_rate=2e-5,
    warmup_steps=500,
    max_steps=50_000,
    logging_steps=50,
    save_steps=2500,
    dataloader_num_workers=4,
    report_to="tensorboard",
)
# With this config, 7B model CPT fits in ~38 GB GPU memory
# (14 GB weights + 7 GB 8-bit optimiser + 17 GB activations)
CPT continues the original CLM training objective from a pre-trained checkpoint using a new data distribution. The loss function does not change; the LR is 10–50× lower than original pre-training.
Use cases include domain injection, code specialisation, language extension, knowledge cutoff updates, and context length extension.
Mix 10–30% general text into the CPT corpus to prevent catastrophic forgetting. Monitor general benchmarks at every checkpoint.
LoRA CPT (r=64, all modules) reduces GPU requirements from ~70 GB to ~8 GB for a 7B model, at the cost of slightly smaller effective domain shift.
Track domain perplexity and general benchmark scores throughout CPT. Stop when perplexity plateaus or general scores degrade beyond 5%.
Notable examples: Code LLaMA (520B code tokens from LLaMA-2), Galactica (106B scientific tokens), BioMedLM (34.6B PubMed tokens from GPT-2).
